title: Incorrect Midpoint Calculation in Binary Search

statement.markdown:
Given a sorted array `nums` of integers, implement a function `binarySearch(nums, target)` that returns the index of the `target` if it exists in the array. If the `target` is not found, return `-1`.

The function should start from the leftmost side of the array and end at the rightmost side. The function should return the index of the target if it is found, otherwise return -1.

The function should use the binary search algorithm to achieve this.

**Notes:**

* The array will not be empty and will be sorted in ascending order.

**Constraints:**

* The length of the array will not exceed `10,000`.
* The array will only contain integers.
* The array will be sorted in ascending order.
statement.inputFormat: Input format is `nums` where `nums` is a sorted array of integers and `target` is an integer.
statement.outputFormat: Output format is an integer which is the index of the target in the array if it exists, otherwise return `-1`.
statement.notes: Time complexity is O(log n) where n is the length of the array. Space complexity is O(1).

examples.explanation: The target is not found in the array.
The target is found in the array at index 0.
The target is found in the array at index 0.

hints.text: The binary search algorithm starts by checking the middle element of the array and compares it to the target.
If the middle element is equal to the target, return the index of the middle element.
If the middle element is greater than the target, the target must be in the left half of the array.

editorial.title: Editorial for Incorrect Midpoint Calculation in Binary Search
editorial.intuition: The binary search algorithm starts by checking the middle element of the array and compares it to the target. If the middle element is greater than the target, the target must be in the left half of the array. If the middle element is less than the target, the target must be in the right half of the array.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach uses a linear search algorithm which has a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.betterSolution: The binary search algorithm has a time complexity of O(log n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses the binary search algorithm with a time complexity of O(log n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the mathematical property that the middle element of the array is within a certain range if the target is within that range.
commonMistakes: Not updating the left and right pointers correctly

functionSignature.python: def binary_search(self, nums: list[int], target: int) -> int:
